EDCS-00381013.text_edition: \n Cereri Augustae ex voto Marcus Romanius Servi filius Voltinia Epulo pro magister publici Cyrenensis\n
EDCS-00381013.translation: To Ceres Augusta, in fulfillment of a vow, Marcus Romanius Epulo, son of Servius, of the Voltinia tribe, in his capacity as *magister publicus* of Cyrene.
EDCS-00381013.summary: This is a votive dedication to Ceres Augusta by a local official in Cyrene, likely dating to the early imperial period.

EDCS-03000837.text_edition: \n Ex auctoritate Neronis Claudi Caesaris Augusti Germanici Livianus proconsul viam colonis coloniae Iuliae Nobilis Cnosi restituit\n
EDCS-03000837.translation: By the authority of Nero Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus, Livianus the proconsul restored the road to the colonists of the colony of Iulia Nobilis Cnossus.
EDCS-03000837.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a road by a proconsul on behalf of the colonists of Cnossus, under the authority of Emperor Nero.

EDCS-05000778.translation: Lucius? Licinius Rufus, son of Lucius.
EDCS-05000778.summary: This is a very short inscription, likely a tombstone or ownership marker, for Lucius Licinius Rufus.

EDCS-08300552.text_edition: \n Quinto Refrio Theagenis filio Collina Theageni decurioni coloniae Cnosiorum honorato quaestura aedilitate IIviratu decreto decurionum Quintus Refrius Theagenes pater\n
EDCS-08300552.translation: To Quintus Refrius Theagenes, son of Theagenes, a decurion of the colony of Cnosos, honored with the quaestorship, aedileship, and the office of *duovir* twice, by decree of the decurions, Quintus Refrius Theagenes his father.
EDCS-08300552.summary: This is a funerary monument set up by a father for his son, a prominent local official in Cnosos, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-08500623.text_edition: \n Caio Sempronio Cai filio Longo duplicario cohortis Hispanorum Venusta liberta\n
EDCS-08500623.translation: To Gaius Sempronius Longus, son of Gaius, a *duplicarius* of the cohort of Spaniards, Venusta his freedwoman.
EDCS-08500623.summary: This inscription records a dedication by a freedwoman to her patron, Gaius Sempronius Longus, who was a soldier in a Spanish cohort.

EDCS-08500624.text_edition: \n Marcus Aemilius Marci filius Macer turma Anici Viani mensor cohortis I Hispanorum annorum XXXX aera XIIX frater hic posuit\n
EDCS-08500624.translation: Marcus Aemilius Macer, son of Marcus, of the turma Anici Viani, a surveyor of the 1st cohort of Spaniards, aged 40, having served 18 years, his brother set up this monument.
EDCS-08500624.summary: This is a tombstone for Marcus Aemilius Macer, a soldier and surveyor in a Spanish cohort, erected by his brother, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-11400055.text_edition: \n tribun-- militum legionis XXXI Publius Munatius Prisco sic Decianus proconsul restituit\n
EDCS-11400055.translation: ..., a military tribune of the 31st Legion, Publius Munatius Priscus, the proconsul Sic Decianus restored this.
EDCS-11400055.summary: This inscription records the restoration of something, possibly a building or road, by the proconsul Sic Decianus, related to a military tribune of the 31st Legion.

EDCS-11400060.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar divi Traiani Parthici filius divi Nervae nepos Traianus Hadrianus Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate consul III pater patriae proconsul pecunia sacra deae Dictunnae fieri iussit XI\n
EDCS-11400060.translation: Emperor Caesar Traianus Hadrianus Augustus, son of the deified Trajan the Parthian, grandson of the deified Nerva, high priest, with tribunician power, consul for the 3rd time, father of the fatherland, proconsul, ordered this sacred precinct of the goddess Dictunna to be made with public money.
EDCS-11400060.summary: This inscription records Emperor Hadrian's order to construct a sacred precinct for the goddess Dictunna using public funds, likely dating to his reign (117-138 AD).

EDCS-12700166.text_edition: \n Titus Pompeius Ligyrus Autrico nnorum XL eques ex cohorte Hispanorum aerum XX hic situs Clemes patrono\n
EDCS-12700166.translation: Titus Pompeius Ligyrus Autricus, aged 40, a cavalryman from the cohort of Spaniards, having served 20 years, is buried here, set up by Clemes, his patron.
EDCS-12700166.summary: This is a tombstone for Titus Pompeius Ligyrus Autricus, a cavalryman in a Spanish cohort, erected by his patron Clemes.

EDCS-13800174.text_edition: \n Iussu Imperatoris Domitiani Caesaris Augusti Germanici pontificis maximi tribunicia potestate VIII patris patriae consulis XIIII censoris perpetui Caius Pomponius Gallus Didius Rufus proconsul locum p<o=V>ssessum a privatis civitati Ptolemaensium restituit \n
EDCS-13800174.translation: By order of Emperor Domitianus Caesar Augustus Germanicus, high priest, with tribunician power for the 8th time, father of the fatherland, consul for the 14th time, censor for life, Caius Pomponius Gallus Didius Rufus, proconsul, restored to the community of the Ptolomaeans the land occupied by private individuals.
EDCS-13800174.summary: This inscription records the restoration of public land to the people of Ptolemaïs by the proconsul Caius Pomponius Gallus Didius Rufus, acting under the authority of Emperor Domitian.

EDCS-20800408.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marcus Aurelius Apollonius veteranus legionis II adiutricis ex doctore fabricae sepulcrum vivus sibi et suis faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-20800408.translation: To the underworld spirits. Marcus Aurelius Apollonius, veteran of the II Adjutrix legion, formerly doctore fabricae, had this tomb made for himself and his family while alive.
EDCS-20800408.summary: A funerary epitaph for a veteran of the II Adjutrix legion, dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24800742.translation: Zosimus, son of Quintus Rupilius Zosimus.
EDCS-24800742.summary: A personal name inscription, possibly a tombstone, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27700097.summary: A personal name inscription, likely a tombstone, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29900152.translation: The most sacred Emperors, Caesars Marcus Aurelius Antoninus and Verus Augusti, Armenian conquerors, Great conquerors of Media, Great conquerors of Parthia, with the water supply, completely ruined everywhere, together with the statues of the princes thrown there, they restored with their generosity, constructing margins on both sides, with marble-encrusted stone, with all its splendor, for the most glorious city of the Gortynians, from the sacred funds of the goddess Dictynna, the procurator of the Augusti, Aelius Apollonius, restored. Dedication. Six days before the Kalends of May, in the consulship of Senecio.
EDCS-29900152.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a public space and statues in Gortyn by the emperors Marcus Aurelius and Lucius Verus, initiated by the procurator Aelius Apollonius and funded by the temple of Dictynna, dated to April 26th in the consulship of Senecio (likely 164 AD).

EDCS-30400025.text_edition: \n Cluatius Cai filius Coachus Cluatius Cai filius Coninus\n
EDCS-30400025.translation: Cluatius Coachus, son of Caius. Cluatius Coninus, son of Caius.
EDCS-30400025.summary: A short inscription recording two personal names, likely a tombstone or a dedication, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-60600072.translation: Emperor Caesar, son of the divine Trajan, Parthicus, nephew of the divine Nerva, Trajan Hadrianus Augustus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 12th time, consul for the 3rd time, father of the country, proconsul, ordered to be made from sacred money for the goddess Dictynna.
EDCS-60600072.summary: An imperial dedication to the goddess Dictynna commissioned by Emperor Hadrian, detailing his titles and powers, likely from the period of his 12th tribunician power and third consulship (around 129 AD).

EDCS-77800201.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, Great Conqueror of Parthia, Great Conqueror of Britannia, Great Conqueror of Germania, Olympian.
EDCS-77800201.summary: A titulary inscription for Emperor Marcus Aurelius, listing his imperial titles and epithets, likely from the period of his conquests in Germania and Britannia (c. 166-169 AD).

EDCS-77800203.translation: Serapion, son of Heracleides, of Alexandria, proxenos and citizen of the Gortynians, himself and his descendants. [6] And L[3], proxenos and citizen of the Gortynians, himself and his descendants. Gaius Lutatius Crispus, soldier, from Ptolemais, proxenos and citizen of the Gortynians, himself and his descendants.
EDCS-77800203.summary: This inscription records honors (proxeny and citizenship) granted by the Gortynians to several individuals, including Serapion of Alexandria and Gaius Lutatius Crispus, a soldier, and their descendants, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-77800204.translation: The council and the people of the Gortynians to Titus Flavius Xenion, the benefactor, under the first official Gaius Terentius Saturnilus. [Inscribed on stone regarding] the distribution of those things left behind in codicils for 8 days. Before the 11th day before the Kalends of May, on the birthday of Rome. Before the 1st day before the Kalends of September, on the birthday of Emperor Caesar Commodus Augustus. Nones of March, on the victory of Emperor Antoninus Augustus the god and Lucilla Augusta. 18 days before the Kalends of January, on the birthday of Lucius Caesar Augustus the god. 11 days before the Kalends of December, on the birthday of Flavius Xenion. Ides of October, on the birthday of Lamprias and Xenophilus. 7 days before the Kalends of August, on the birthday of Zenophilus. 1 day before the Kalends of August, on the birthday of Claudia Marcelline.
EDCS-77800204.summary: This inscription details the distribution of property by Titus Flavius Xenion, a benefactor of the Gortynians, and lists several festival dates associated with imperial birthdays and the birthdays of other individuals, likely from the late 2nd century AD.

EDCS-77800205.translation: Master of all mankind in the world, Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Valerius Maximianus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, lord of himself and the world. Marcus Aurelius Byzes, the most distinguished governor of Crete.
EDCS-77800205.summary: An inscription recording the erection of a monument by Marcus Aurelius Byzes, governor of Crete, to Emperor Maximian, likely from the Tetrarchic period (late 3rd to early 4th century AD).

EDCS-78000035.translation: To Marcus Roscius Kyreinas Lepidus Murena, son of Marcus Murena, who held the office of strategos, grandson of Marcus Murena, former proconsul of Bithynia, holder of the septimvirate, chiliarch of the Seventh Claudian Legion, treasurer and proconsul of the province of Crete and Cyrene, Volumnia Calida, his wife, dedicated this.
EDCS-78000035.summary: This inscription is a dedication by Volumnia Calida to her husband, Marcus Roscius Kyreinas Lepidus Murena, detailing his prestigious career in Roman administration and military service, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-78000036.translation: In the 16th year, under the supervision of the grain supply for the Romans, having been honored with the priesthood of the 15 [---]rians in Italy, Legate of Africa, Proconsul, [---] five-banded of Raetia, Volumnia Calida, his dearest and most pious son.
EDCS-78000036.summary: This fragmentary inscription commemorates a distinguished individual, likely a son of Volumnia Calida, who held high offices including legate of Africa and proconsul, with his priesthood and military distinctions noted, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-78300556.translation: Gnaeus Papirius Actius, the most distinguished treasurer and proconsul, [dedicated this] to Marcus Claudius Charmosynus Pratoneicus, the former high priest of the community of the Cretans, his fellow student and personal benefactor.
EDCS-78300556.summary: This inscription is a dedication by Marcus Claudius Charmosynus Pratoneicus to Gnaeus Papirius Actius, honoring him for his service as treasurer and proconsul and acknowledging their past association as fellow students and benefactor-beneficiary relationship, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-78300558.translation: To Aulus Larcius Kyreinas Lepidus Sulpicianus, son of Aulus Larcius Gallus, a senator, the most pious and dearest son. Sulpicia Telerō, his mother, decreed this, with the approval of the most distinguished council of Gortyn.
EDCS-78300558.summary: This inscription is a dedication by Sulpicia Telerō to her son, Aulus Larcius Kyreinas Lepidus Sulpicianus, honoring him as a senator and a devoted son, with the decree made by the council of Gortyn, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-78300603.translation: In the 15th year, during the priesthood of Philiskos son of Euphanes, while Caesar Augustus, son of a god, was emperor, Barkaios Theochrestos [and the] nomophylakes (guardians of the law): Apollonios son of Euippos, Theodorus son of Ariston, Apollonios son of Eudaimon, Artemidoros son of Hagemachos, Ammonios son of Sophanes, Menandros son of Antipatro, Serapion son of Polymnastos, Hyratthis daughter of Dionysios, Alexandros son of Alexandros, dedicated Aphrodite Nomophylakida.
EDCS-78300603.summary: This inscription records the dedication of the cult image of Aphrodite Nomophylakida by the nomophylakes (guardians of the law) of Barkaios, during the 15th year of an unspecified reign and the priesthood of Philiskos, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-78400118.translation: In the first year of the emperors Marcus Aurelius Antoninus and Lucius Aurelius Verus, Gaius Claudius Titianus Demostratus, proconsul of Crete and Cyrene, the metropolis, dedicated this.
EDCS-78400118.summary: This inscription records the dedication by Gaius Claudius Titianus Demostratus, proconsul of Crete and Cyrene, during the first year of the joint reign of Marcus Aurelius and Lucius Verus, dating it to 161 CE.

EDCS-78500074.summary: This is a short dedication to a divine Lucius, likely a deified Roman emperor or an important local figure, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-78500082.translation: It has 'Elpidas' from this column to the basilica.
EDCS-78500082.summary: This inscription appears to be a boundary marker or directional sign, indicating a distance of 'Elpidas' (likely a unit of measurement or a person's name) from a column to a basilica, probably from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-78700112.translation: To Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Felix Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus Olympius, and to Iulia Augusta, Mother of Castra.
EDCS-78700112.summary: This is a dedication or titulature inscription honoring Emperor Septimius Severus and Empress Julia Domna, listing their extensive titles and epithets, likely dating to their joint rule in the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-78700113.translation: To Emperor Caesar, son of the god Nerva, Nerva Trajanus Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, pontifex maximus, holder of tribunician power for the 17th year, consul for the 6th year, father of the fatherland, founder of the inhabited world. The city of the Lyttians, through the protokosmos Marcus Pompeius C(l)eumenidas, dedicated this.
EDCS-78700113.summary: This inscription records the dedication by the city of Lyttos to Emperor Trajan, listing his imperial titles and honors, and the official who oversaw the dedication, dating it to Trajan's 17th year of tribunician power (113/114 CE).

EDCS-78700114.translation: Of Emperor Gaius Caesar Augustus Germanicus.
EDCS-78700114.summary: This is an inscription honouring Gaius Caesar Augustus Germanicus, likely Caligula, possibly part of a titulature on a public monument.

EDCS-79000108.inscription_type: tituli honorarii, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-79000108.translation: For the good fortune, victory, and endurance of the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus and his whole house. Tiberius Claudius Battus, the priest, repaired and roofed the temple of Eisidos from the revenues of Apollo, during the proconsulship of the most excellent proconsul Numisius Marcellianus.
EDCS-79000108.summary: This inscription records the repair and roofing of the temple of Eisidos by the priest Tiberius Claudius Battus, funded by Apollo's revenues, during the proconsulship of Numisius Marcellianus, likely in the reign of Marcus Aurelius.

EDCS-79000109.inscription_type: tituli honorarii, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-79000109.translation: For the good fortune, victory, and endurance of the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus and his whole house. Tiberius Claudius Battus, the priest, repaired and roofed the temple of the Nymphagetes from the revenues of Apollo, during the proconsulship of the most excellent proconsul Numisius Marcellianus.
EDCS-79000109.summary: This inscription records the repair and roofing of the temple of the Nymphagetes by the priest Tiberius Claudius Battus, funded by Apollo's revenues, during the proconsulship of Numisius Marcellianus, likely in the reign of Marcus Aurelius.

EDCS-82900103.translation: God, good fortune. Sacred to Apollo. Tiberius Claudius Epicrates, son of Asclepiades.
EDCS-82900103.summary: This is a dedicatory inscription to Apollo by Tiberius Claudius Epicrates, son of Asclepiades, likely on a religious monument.

EDCS-09001570.translation: Gaius Clodius Vestalis, son of Gaius, proconsul, restored the August water. Vestalis, proconsul, restored the August water.
EDCS-09001570.summary: This bilingual inscription records the restoration of a water supply (aqueduct or channel) by the proconsul Gaius Clodius Vestalis, likely in a provincial setting.

EDCS-09200544.translation: Marcus Sufenas Proculus had this made for Tiberius Caesar, son of Augustus, Imperator, holder of tribunician power.
EDCS-09200544.summary: This inscription records a commission by Marcus Sufenas Proculus for Tiberius Caesar, likely related to a public work or monument during Tiberius' reign.

EDCS-09301283.translation: Lucius Corona, son of Lucius, Pro, proconsul had this made. Us trib.
EDCS-09301283.summary: This inscription records that Lucius Corona Pro, a proconsul, commissioned a work, with the remaining text possibly indicating the authority or purpose.

EDCS-09401230.translation: To Bruttidius Sabinus, Augustus, proconsul, in the name of the Cyrenaicans and the Roman citizens who do business there. Titus S... Celer, prefect of a cohort.
EDCS-09401230.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the proconsul Bruttidius Sabinus, sponsored by the people of Cyrene and Roman merchants, with Titus S... Celer, a cohort prefect, also mentioned.

EDCS-09401233.translation: Publius Blaesius Rusticus, son of Publius, aged 3 years. Publius Blaesius Rusticus, father, aged 28 years.
EDCS-09401233.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the death of a young child, Publius Blaesius Rusticus, and the age of his father, also named Publius Blaesius Rusticus.

EDCS-09401234.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the death of Lucius Foficanus Niger at the age of 87.

EDCS-09401235.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Traianus Hadrianus Augustus, son of the deified Trajan the Parthian, grandson of the deified Nerva, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the second time, consul for the second time, from the community of the Cyrenes.
EDCS-09401235.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Emperor Hadrian, likely set up by the community of Cyrene in the Roman province of Cyrenaica, dating to his early reign.

EDCS-09401236.translation: Titus Aelius Democritus, son of Titus, of the Palatine tribe, a Roman knight, for the health of his sons Iulius Saturninus and Iulius Catullinus, to Aesculapius and Hygieia.
EDCS-09401236.summary: This is a private votive offering to the gods Aesculapius and Hygieia by Titus Aelius Democritus for the well-being of his sons, dating likely to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-09401596.translation: The Emperor Caesar Nerva Traianus Augustus Germanicus, son of the deified Nerva, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the fourth time, consul for the third time, father of the country, made the road through the selected recruits in the province of Cyrene, 8 miles.
EDCS-09401596.summary: This inscription records the construction of a road by Emperor Trajan, undertaken by legionary recruits in the province of Cyrenaica, dating to his reign.

EDCS-09401597.translation: The Emperor Caesar Marcus Iulius Philippus Pius Felix Invictus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power, consul, proconsul, father of the country, and Marcus Iulius Philippus, the most noble Caesar, consul, [restored] 10 miles from Cyrene.
EDCS-09401597.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a road by Emperor Philip the Arab and his son, Caesar Philip, dating to their joint rule in the 3rd century AD.

EDCS-09401598.translation: To our Lords, Flavius Valerius Constantinus Augustus and Flavius Claudius Constantinus, Flavius Valerius Constantinus, the most noble Caesars.
EDCS-09401598.summary: This is an acclamation or dedication to the emperors Constantine the Great and his sons, dating to the Tetrarchic period or early 4th century AD.

EDCS-09701225.translation: Under Emperor Domitian Caesar Augustus Germanicus, in his tenth consulship, between the colony Flavia Augusta Felix Capua and Plotium Plebeium, according to the judgment of Emperor Titus Augustus, and also by decree of the colony of Capua, by agreement of both parties, boundary stones were placed, with Publius Messius Campanus serving as procurator of the Campanians.
EDCS-09701225.summary: This inscription records the establishment of boundary stones between two settlements in Italy, mediated by imperial officials and decrees, dating to the reign of Domitian.

EDCS-09701226.translation: To Gaius Pomponius Cordius, procurator of our Augusti, Valerius Valens, prefect of the I Macedonica cohort, [sets this up as a tribute] to the best and most benevolent governor.
EDCS-09701226.summary: This is a dedication by a military prefect to his governor, Valerius Valens to Gaius Pomponius Cordius, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-09701227.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Antonius Gordianus Pius Felix Augustus, pontifex maximus, the Gordian I Macedonica cavalry cohort, devoted to his divine majesty, dedicated this, with Caecilius Felix, a distinguished man, serving as his procurator, under the supervision of Drusinius Lupus, prefect of the same cohort, as an evocatus.
EDCS-09701227.summary: This inscription is a dedication by the Gordian I Macedonica cohort to Emperor Gordian III, likely set up by its prefect and procurator, dating to his reign in the 3rd century AD.

EDCS-09701228.translation: A detachment of the III Augusta legion, under the supervision of Aurelius Mucianus, dux.
EDCS-09701228.summary: This inscription records the activity of a military detachment from the III Augusta legion, led by a dux, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-09701706.translation: Vestalis, proconsul, saw to the repair of the portico of Augustus.
EDCS-09701706.summary: This inscription records the repair of the portico of Augustus, commissioned by the proconsul Vestalis, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-09701708.translation: The proconsul arranged for this to be made at his own expense.
EDCS-09701708.summary: This inscription records a public work commissioned and funded by a proconsul, likely in the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-09701710.translation: Friends of the emperor to the prefect of the cohort.
EDCS-09701710.summary: This inscription is a dedication from friends of the emperor to a cohort prefect, possibly relating to military matters.

EDCS-09701712.translation: Sextarius exacted by Quintus, by order of our Emperors and Caesars.
EDCS-09701712.summary: This inscription records a financial or administrative measure concerning a 'sextarius' and ordered by emperors, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-09800347.translation: By authority of the Emperor Caesar Vespasian Augustus, Gaius Arinius Modestus, proconsul, divided and assigned in perpetuity to Apollonius, son of Paraebates, the choria of the Apolloniatans, which the state had purchased with its allies, for an annual payment of 408 denarii, guaranteed by Theodorus, son of Theodorus, and Antonius Bathyllus, of which there are 23 medimni and a half-medimnus.
EDCS-09800347.summary: This inscription from the reign of Vespasian records a proconsul's division and permanent lease of public land to a specific individual, detailing the terms of the lease and guarantees.

EDCS-11400043.summary: A short dedication to Emperor Augustus, likely from the early imperial period.

EDCS-11400045.translation: To the temple attendant, the flamen of the deified Vespasian, the duovir, and patron of the colony.
EDCS-11400045.summary: This inscription honors a local official who held multiple religious and civic positions, including flamen of Vespasian and duovir, suggesting a date after Vespasian's deification (AD 79).

EDCS-11400049.translation: The aedile Cianus made this sacred offering.
EDCS-11400049.summary: This brief inscription records a sacred offering made by an aedile, a type of Roman magistrate responsible for public buildings and games.

EDCS-11400051.summary: This inscription simply names Caesar Augustus, likely a formal dedication or part of a larger monument.

EDCS-11400056.translation: By authority of Nero Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 11th time, imperator for the 4th time, consul for the 4th time, father of the country, and by decree of the Senate, Lucius Turpilius Dexter, proconsul, restored and delimited the public lands of the Gortynians, many of which had been occupied by private individuals.
EDCS-11400056.summary: This inscription records a proconsul's action under Nero to restore and demarcate public lands that had been illegally occupied, demonstrating the exercise of Roman authority in a province.

EDCS-11400061.translation: From Caesar Augustus and Titus Vibius Varus
EDCS-11400061.summary: This inscription mentions both Caesar Augustus and Titus Vibius Varus, likely indicating a joint dedication or commission, possibly related to imperial projects.

EDCS-11400064.translation: Emperor Caesar, son of the divine [Augustus], [---]
EDCS-11400064.summary: This is a fragment of an inscription likely honoring Emperor Augustus, the son of Julius Caesar, and dates to his reign (27 BCE - 14 CE).

EDCS-11400069.translation: To Diocletian and Maximian, unconquered Augusti, the lights of his world; Aglaus, proconsul.
EDCS-11400069.summary: This inscription honors the emperors Diocletian and Maximian, dedicated by the proconsul Aglaus, likely dating to their joint rule (286-305 CE).

EDCS-11400071.summary: This is a short inscription identifying Lucius Plotius Vicina as a proconsul.

EDCS-11400072.translation: merchants of Doia, daughter of Lucius, Procilla, Roman citizens who trade in Gortyna.
EDCS-11400072.summary: This inscription lists Roman citizens who are merchants in Gortyna, identifying Doia and Procilla.

EDCS-11400074.translation: To Marcus Sonteius, son of Marcus, of the Teretina tribe, of Casina, priest of the deified Augustus, four times aedile and duumvir of the colony of Cnosia, augur of the same colony, by his pupils Publeilius, son of Caius, Campanus, and Procilla and Tertulla, defended by him for honor's sake.
EDCS-11400074.summary: This is a dedication to Marcus Sonteius, a prominent official in the colony of Cnosia, honoring his various civic and religious roles and mentioning his pupils who sponsored it, likely dating to the late 1st century BCE or early 1st century CE.

EDCS-11400075.summary: This inscription records the restoration of something by Vestalis, who held the office of proconsul.

EDCS-11400076.translation: Lucius Plotius Vicina, proconsul, made [it] at his own expense.
EDCS-11400076.summary: This inscription states that Lucius Plotius Vicina, in his capacity as proconsul, funded and constructed something at his own expense.

EDCS-11400077.summary: This is a brief inscription identifying Proculus as a proconsul.

EDCS-11400078.translation: Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Nerva, Trajan Augustus Germanicus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 4th time, father of the fatherland, consul for the 3rd time; he restored the odeon of the community of the Gortynians, which had collapsed in ruins, under the supervision of Lucius Elufrius Severus, proconsul.
EDCS-11400078.summary: This inscription records Emperor Trajan's restoration of a public building (odeon) in Gortyna, completed under the supervision of the proconsul Lucius Elufrius Severus, and dates to Trajan's reign (98-117 CE).

EDCS-11400081.translation: Son of Marcus, son of Collina, of the Romilia tribe.
EDCS-11400081.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription providing gentilician and tribal affiliation, likely part of a longer personal dedication or record.

EDCS-11400082.summary: This inscription is a fragment mentioning family relationships, possibly part of a funerary monument.

EDCS-11400083.summary: This is a fragment that likely refers to the emperor Tiberius, possibly part of a larger inscription or dedication.

EDCS-11400084.summary: This inscription fragment likely records the name of Publius Clodius Proculus, possibly in a dedication or ownership context.

EDCS-11400085.summary: This inscription fragment seems to indicate origin and ownership, possibly related to a property or a person named Crescentis.

EDCS-13302464.translation: Emperor Caesar Nerva Trajan, son of the deified [Nerva], Augustus, Germanicus, chief priest, holding tribunician power, father of the country, consul, built a bathhouse and baths through Gaius Memmius
EDCS-13302464.summary: This is a dedication by Emperor Trajan for the construction of a bathhouse, likely from the early 2nd century CE.

EDCS-13302504.translation: to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, by a proconsul
EDCS-13302504.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by a proconsul, likely from a Roman province.

EDCS-13302507.translation: Marcus Sufenas Proculus had it restored
EDCS-13302507.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a building or monument by Marcus Sufenas Proculus.

EDCS-13302608.translation: forever victorious and triumphant always Augusti, the most perfect governor decided and dedicated their eternal foundation
EDCS-13302608.summary: This inscription records a decision by a governor concerning the perpetual status of some group or entity.

EDCS-13900501.translation: to Gaius Julius Caro, son of Gaius, of the Voltinian tribe, from the province of Narbonensis, tribune of soldiers of the III Cyrenaica Legion, prefect of the II Asturum equitata cohort, decorated in the British War with a mural crown, a vallary crown, a golden crown, and a pure spear, by the centurions and soldiers of the III Cyrenaica Legion and of the XXII Legion sent into the province of Cyrene for conscription
EDCS-13900501.summary: This inscription honors a military officer, Gaius Julius Caro, detailing his career and decorations, likely from the late 1st or early 2nd century CE.

EDCS-13900591.translation: Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus, chief priest, holding tribunician power, emperor for the 11th time, father of the country, consul for the 3rd time, designated consul for the 4th time, restored [this] in the year of Caesar Veientonius, the proconsul, year 1
EDCS-13900591.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a monument by Emperor Claudius, dated to a specific consular year, likely from the mid-1st century CE.

EDCS-13900592.translation: Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrian Augustus, son of the deified Trajan Parthicus, grandson of the deified Nerva, chief priest, with tribunician power for the second time, consul for the third time, restored the road, which had been overthrown and corrupted by the tumult of the Jews, through the soldiers of the cohort.
EDCS-13900592.summary: This inscription records Emperor Hadrian restoring a road damaged during the Jewish revolt, likely erected in Judea during his reign (117-138 CE).

EDCS-14800213.translation: Lentolus, son of Lentulus, priest of Apollo, when Alexsis Alexandri, who trade in Cyrene, acted as prior, and also the region of the Cyrenaeans, Alexsis son of Alexandri, who loved the Romans and agreed with them, and who was never alienated from them, and indeed has now also...
EDCS-14800213.summary: This fragmented inscription from Cyrene, possibly from the Roman period, seems to concern a decree or recognition involving local traders named Alexsis Alexandri and their relationship with Rome and the region.

EDCS-15300301.translation: Porticus of Caesar. Marcus P. Pacileus.
EDCS-15300301.summary: This very short inscription likely records the construction or dedication of a portico (covered walkway) associated with Caesar, possibly funded or commissioned by Marcus P. Pacileus, during the early Imperial period.

EDCS-15300302.translation: To Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrian Augustus, son of the deified Trajan Parthicus, grandson of the deified Nerva, chief priest, with tribunician power for the second time, consul for the second time, by the city of the Cyrenaeans. To Imperator Caesar, son of the deified Trajan Parthicus, grandson of Nerva, Trajan Hadrian.
EDCS-15300302.summary: This bilingual (Latin and Greek) inscription from Cyrene dedicates a monument or building to Emperor Hadrian, commissioned by the city itself, likely during his reign (117-138 CE).

EDCS-15600020.translation: Gaius Clodius Vestalis, son of Caius, proconsul, restored the Aqua Augusta.
EDCS-15600020.summary: This inscription records Gaius Clodius Vestalis, a proconsul, restoring the Aqua Augusta (an aqueduct), likely in the province where he served, probably during the late Republican or early Imperial period.

EDCS-15800300.translation: Nero Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus, son of the deified Claudius, grandson of Germanicus Caesar, great-grandson of Tiberius Caesar Augustus, great-great-grandson of the deified Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power, emperor, consul, through his legate Lucius Acilius Strabo, restored to the Roman people lands occupied by private individuals.
EDCS-15800300.summary: This inscription records Emperor Nero, through his legate Lucius Acilius Strabo, restoring public lands that had been illegally occupied by private individuals, likely during his reign (54-68 CE).

EDCS-15800301.translation: Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrian Augustus, son of the deified Trajan Parthicus, grandson of the deified Nerva, chief priest, with tribunician power for the second time, consul for the third time, restored the road, which had been overthrown and corrupted by the tumult of the Jews, through Q[...]
EDCS-15800301.summary: This inscription, similar to EDCS-13900592 but incomplete, records Emperor Hadrian restoring a road damaged during the Jewish revolt, likely erected in Judea during his reign (117-138 CE).

EDCS-15800303.translation: Emperor Caesar Vespasian Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the third time, emperor for the eighth time, father of the fatherland, consul for the third time, designated consul for the fourth time, through his legate Quintus Paconius Agrippinus, restored Ptolemaeus to the Roman people.
EDCS-15800303.summary: This inscription records Emperor Vespasian, through his legate Quintus Paconius Agrippinus, restoring a person or property named Ptolemaeus to the Roman people, likely during his reign (69-79 CE).

EDCS-15800304.translation: Emperor Caesar Vespasian Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the third time, emperor for the eighth time, father of the fatherland, consul for the third time, designated consul for the fourth time, through his legate Quintus Paconius Agrippinus, restored Ptolemaeus to the Roman people. Imperator Caesar Vespasianus, high priest, great high priest, with the power of a tribune for the third time, emperor for the eighth time, father of the fatherland, consul for the third time, designated for the fourth time, through Quintus Paconius Agrippinus, his own legate, restored Ptolemaeus to the Roman people.
EDCS-15800304.summary: This bilingual (Latin and Greek) inscription records Emperor Vespasian, through his legate Quintus Paconius Agrippinus, restoring a person or property named Ptolemaeus to the Roman people, likely during his reign (69-79 CE).

EDCS-15800305.translation: Emperor Caesar Vespasian Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the third time, emperor for the eighth time, father of the fatherland, consul for the fourth time, designated consul for the fifth time, through his legate Quintus Paconius, restored the garden to the Roman people.
EDCS-15800305.summary: This inscription records Emperor Vespasian, through his legate Quintus Paconius, restoring a garden to the Roman people, likely during his reign (69-79 CE).

EDCS-15800306.translation: Quintus Aemilius Valens, a veteran of the Third Cohort, from an honorable discharge.
EDCS-15800306.summary: This inscription records a veteran named Quintus Aemilius Valens, detailing his service in the Third Cohort and honorable discharge.

EDCS-16000495.translation: Under Emperor Caesar Augustus, chief priest, Quintus Lucanius Proculus, proconsul, saw to it that the walls of the citadel were repaired.
EDCS-16000495.summary: This inscription records Quintus Lucanius Proculus, proconsul, undertaking the repair of a citadel's walls during the reign of Emperor Augustus.

EDCS-16100250.translation: Lucius Plotius Vicina, proconsul, made this from his own funds.
EDCS-16100250.summary: This inscription records Lucius Plotius Vicina, a proconsul, funding and constructing something at his own expense.

EDCS-16200649.translation: Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrian Augustus, son of the divine Trajan the Parthian, grandson of the divine Nerva, chief priest, with tribunician power for the third time, consul for the third time, ordered that the baths with their porticoes and gymnasia and other adjacent facilities, which had been destroyed and burned in the Jewish tumult, be restored to the community of Cyrene.
EDCS-16200649.summary: This inscription records Emperor Hadrian restoring public baths and related facilities to the community of Cyrene after damage sustained during the Jewish revolt, around 117-118 AD.

EDCS-16201606.translation: Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrian Augustus, son of the divine Trajan the Parthian, grandson of the divine Nerva, chief priest, with tribunician power for the second time, consul for the third time, restored the road, which had been demolished and ruined in the Jewish tumult, using soldiers.
EDCS-16201606.summary: This inscription records Emperor Hadrian using soldiers to restore a road damaged during the Jewish revolt, likely around 118 AD.

EDCS-16201997.translation: oni, augur, co[n] Mam. Pinianus, procurator.
EDCS-16201997.summary: This inscription appears to record an augur and a procurator named Pinianus, with partial names for other individuals.

EDCS-20800405.translation: To Jupiter Sol Optimus Maximus Sarapis and all the gods and to Emperor Caesar Nerva Trajan Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, Epictetus, freedman courier, with the work being managed by Dionysius, son of Sostratos of Alexandria, governor of the ship Parasemo Isopharia, belonging to Titus Claudius Theon.
EDCS-20800405.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter, Sarapis, and the Emperor Trajan by Epictetus, a freedman courier, for a ship managed by Dionysius and belonging to Titus Claudius Theon, likely from the early 2nd century AD.

EDCS-20800406.translation: To Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pertinax Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the third time, consul for the second time, general for the third time, father of the fatherland, the Roman citizens residing in Gortyna, from the sum which he contributed for his decurionate, Flavius Titianus, designated priest of the divine Trajan, with the supervision of Lucius Naevius Exacesta, priest of the divine Augustus, and all the Roman citizens residing in Gortyna.
EDCS-20800406.summary: This inscription records Roman citizens in Gortyna making a contribution related to their decurionate and honors Emperor Septimius Severus, with Flavius Titianus and Lucius Naevius Exacesta playing prominent roles, likely from around 193-195 AD.

EDCS-20800407.translation: Quintus Volteios Menodoros, freedman of Quintus; Eros, son of Marcus P. Lucius.
EDCS-20800407.summary: This inscription records two individuals, Quintus Volteios Menodoros and Eros, identifying their patron and father respectively.

EDCS-20800409.translation: Urbasia Dio, freedwoman of Titus, for herself and her family.
EDCS-20800409.summary: This inscription records Urbasia Dio, a freedwoman, dedicating something for herself and her household.

EDCS-20800410.translation: To Julia Augusta by the people of Cyrene, under the proconsul Publius Octavius.
EDCS-20800410.summary: This is a dedication to Julia Augusta, wife of Augustus, set up by the citizens of Cyrene during the proconsulship of Publius Octavius.

EDCS-20800412.translation: Pacilaeus, son of Marcus, restored the imperial colonnade.
EDCS-20800412.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a public portico by Pacilaeus, son of Marcus, likely during the imperial period.

EDCS-20800413.translation: Lucius Numisius Marius, freedman of Lucius, had this made for his brother Lucius Vibius Gattabus, freedman of Lucius.
EDCS-20800413.summary: This is a funerary or dedication text recording a commission by Lucius Numisius Marius for his brother Lucius Vibius Gattabus, both freedmen, likely from the early imperial period.

EDCS-20900083.summary: This is a very brief inscription, possibly a name and a numeral, possibly related to a property or a person named Publius Ancharius Collina.

EDCS-29100260.translation: Publius Viriasius Naso, in his third proconsulship, consecrated this at his own expense to the divine powers and providence of Tiberius Caesar Augustus and his senate, on the day which was the fifteenth before the Kalends of November.
EDCS-29100260.summary: This inscription records the consecration of a monument by Publius Viriasius Naso, a proconsul, to the divine powers and providence of Emperor Tiberius and the Senate, funded by himself on a specific date.

EDCS-29100261.summary: This is a simple dedication to Julia Augusta, wife of Emperor Augustus.

EDCS-29100262.translation: Fortunatianus Servilius, a most distinguished consular man, saw to it. Fortunatianus Servilius, a most distinguished consular man, saw to it.
EDCS-29100262.summary: This inscription records that Fortunatianus Servilius, a high-ranking official (consularis), supervised or commissioned a work, with the statement repeated for emphasis.

EDCS-29601627.translation: To Caesar, son of the divine one, Augustus.
EDCS-29601627.summary: This is a dedication to Emperor Augustus, referring to him as the son of the deified Julius Caesar.

EDCS-30100774.translation: Nero Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus restored to the colony of Julia Cnossus, through Publius Licinius Secundus, procurator, five jugera of land that had been given by the deified Augustus and confirmed by the deified Claudius.
EDCS-30100774.summary: This inscription details the restoration of five jugera of land in the colony of Julia Cnossus by Emperor Nero, confirming a grant originally made by Augustus and confirmed by Claudius, with the transaction overseen by the procurator Publius Licinius Secundus.

EDCS-31300089.translation: To Marcus Roscius Quirinus Luppus Murena, son of Marcus Murena, who held the office of strategos, grandson of Marcus Murena, proconsul of Bithynia, a diligent centurion of the Seventh Claudian Legion, a patron of the Fourth Flavian Legion, treasurer and proconsul of the province of Crete and Cyrene, Volumnia Calida, wife of his granddaughter, dedicated this.
EDCS-31300089.summary: This is a dedication by Volumnia Calida to her husband's granddaughter's husband, Marcus Roscius Quirinus Luppus Murena, detailing his extensive career in the Roman military and administration, likely from the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-31300090.translation: During the time of the office of the grain measurer of the Romans, having been honored with the priesthood of the 15 priests from Italy, ambassador to Africa, proconsul, and holder of the lictors' rods, for Raetia, Volumnia Calida dedicated this to her most beloved and most pious son.
EDCS-31300090.summary: This inscription records a dedication by Volumnia Calida to her son, highlighting his distinguished career involving religious office, ambassadorship, proconsulship, and service in Raetia, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-31300174.translation: To King Ptolemy Philopator and Queen Arsinoe, the water supply and the nymphaeum, by Lucius Gaius, a Roman, commander of the garrison.
EDCS-31300174.summary: This is a dedication of a water supply and nymphaeum to King Ptolemy Philopator and Queen Arsinoe by Lucius Gaius, a Roman garrison commander, probably from the Hellenistic period in Egypt.

EDCS-31500358.translation: To the slave-girl, daughter of Publius, PV.
EDCS-31500358.summary: This is a simple funerary inscription for a slave-girl, identified as the daughter of Publius, dating from the Roman Republic or early Empire.

EDCS-32500002.summary: This is a dedication or dedication to Tiberius Caesar, likely from the early Roman Empire.

EDCS-44200067.translation: Emperor Nerva Trajanus Caesar Augustus Germanicus, son of the deified Nerva, chief priest, with tribunician power for the third time, father of the country, consul for the second time, dedicated this.
EDCS-44200067.summary: This is an imperial dedication by Emperor Trajan, commemorating his titles and offices, including consulships and tribunician power, and likely marking the completion of a public work, dating to around 100 CE.

EDCS-51000137.translation: To Diocletian, the Pious, Fortunate, Invincible Emperor.
EDCS-51000137.summary: This is an honorific inscription to Emperor Diocletian, emphasizing his titles and divine attributes, likely from the late 3rd or early 4th century CE.

EDCS-55701618.summary: This inscription is a repetition of the title 'Emperor Caesar', possibly an incomplete dedication or a repetition for emphasis, dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-55701620.summary: This is a simple inscription bearing the name of Gnaeus Ateius Rufus, possibly a tombstone or a dedication, from the Roman Republic or early Empire.

EDCS-55701623.summary: This inscription simply states the name Clodius Proculus, likely a funerary marker or ownership inscription, dating from the Roman Republic or early Empire.

EDCS-55701627.summary: This inscription is simply the name Lucius Militius, likely a funerary or ownership marker.

EDCS-55701629.summary: This inscription is the full name Lucius Rasinius Pisanus, probably part of a funerary monument or an individual's identification.

EDCS-55701630.summary: This inscription records the name Chryseros, son of Lucius Titius, likely a personal name on a tomb or property.

EDCS-57800057.translation: Sacred to Libya, for the safety of Publius Pomponius Secundus, proconsul. Messius Atticus, centurion.
EDCS-57800057.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Libya by the centurion Messius Atticus for the safety of the proconsul Publius Pomponius Secundus, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-59800072.translation: Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Nerva, Nerva Traianus
EDCS-59800072.summary: This inscription is the full titulature of Emperor Trajan, identifying him as the son of the deified Nerva, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-66300049.translation: To Emperor Nerva Trajan Caesar Augustus Germanicus Dacicus
EDCS-66300049.summary: This inscription is a dedication in Greek to Emperor Trajan, listing his imperial titles including Germanicus and Dacicus, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-67200391.translation: For the fortune and safety of our lord Emperor [---] Trajan Caesar Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, Chrysōn, son of Ateimetos, for his devotion which he promised as first kosmos of the city of Cherson, dedicated the temple together with the statue and the portico, together with Sōtēria his wife and children, from his own funds.
EDCS-67200391.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a temple, statue, and portico by Chrysōn, a local official, for the well-being of Emperor Trajan and his family, likely from the late 1st or early 2nd century CE.

EDCS-67200393.translation: Apellas, son of Patron, and Rhoda, his wife, both lie here under the earth. All things concerning them are good and pleasing to all citizens and foreigners, and desired. But you, O city of Camara, rejoice and flourish always, and may we too rejoice if they rejoice when dead.
EDCS-67200393.summary: This is a Greek epitaph for Apellas and his wife Rhoda, expressing their positive reputation and wishing well to the city of Camara and the deceased, likely from the Hellenistic or Roman period.

EDCS-67200394.translation: Lord, Zosime, your servant, who was received by your council, lies here. Place her, Lord, in a place of rest, who was faithful, devoted to her husband, devoted to her children, and fled to you, Lord, the only merciful one. She rested in Christ, in memory of Saint John.
EDCS-67200394.summary: This is an early Christian inscription, a prayer for the servant Zosime, asking for rest and mercy in Christ, likely from the 4th-5th century CE.

EDCS-67200395.translation: Cosmianē, having adorned herself in this world, rests in Christ and lies here.
EDCS-67200395.summary: This is a Christian epitaph for Cosmianē, stating she lived in this world for five years and now rests in Christ, likely from the late Roman or early Byzantine period.

EDCS-67200396.translation: Onesimus, a man most faithful to Christ, lies here, aged 54.
EDCS-67200396.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Onesimus, a Christian man, dating to the 4th or 5th century AD.

EDCS-67200397.translation: Truly pious and God-loving Paul, a presbyter of the holy church of God here, having finished his life with reverence and led a good life, leaving earthly things, you were united with Christ who travels the heavens, holy father, you were perfected in peace. Died in the month of October, year 36 of the 6th indiction, aged 55.
EDCS-67200397.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Paul, a presbyter of the church, recording his death in October, year 36 of the 6th indiction, at the age of 55.

EDCS-67200398.translation: Persis rests here, a servant of Christ, noble by birth and free in her ways, having completed her earthly life with reverence. Rested in the month of May, year 16 of the 2nd indiction.
EDCS-67200398.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Persis, a servant of Christ, noble by birth, who died in May, year 16 of the 2nd indiction.

EDCS-67200399.translation: In my seventy-third year I trod the fertile earth, and now I lie here, Gerasimus, a man pleasing to all, owing nothing to anyone, and now, being a servant of Christ, I have sought refuge in the Lord.
EDCS-67200399.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Gerasimus, who lived to be 73 and died as a servant of Christ.

EDCS-67200400.translation: Have mercy on me, Lord. I am dead, and my body Crete held. Sambatius is my name, being a servant of Christ. Be saved and pray, singing praises to the Triune God.
EDCS-67200400.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Sambatius, a servant of Christ, requesting prayers from the living.

EDCS-67200401.translation: Eutropia, the sweet, pure virgin, a Christ-bearer of nine years, lies here. Mary gives birth to Christ. Age 19.
EDCS-67200401.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Eutropia, a nine-year-old virgin and 'Christ-bearer', who died at age 19.

EDCS-67200402.translation: Jesus Christ, remember Petronia who lies here, a woman of great renown. This tomb has covered her, drawing down the whole earth.
EDCS-67200402.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Petronia, a renowned woman, requesting remembrance from Jesus Christ.

EDCS-67200403.translation: Theodotimus, son of Gennous, an official, and Theotima, lie here.
EDCS-67200403.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Theodotimus, son of Gennous and Theotima.

EDCS-67200404.translation: Glory to Christ. Lord Jesus Christ, remember your servant Sambatida. I lived with a good husband and now I lie here, ordering all things well, having taken the writings of Christ to heart, who has given me children, and I have left these to you. Live and pray, always having God merciful.
EDCS-67200404.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Sambatida, a servant of Christ and mother, who lived a good life and instructs the living to pray.

EDCS-67200405.translation: Zosime, a servant of Christ, a widow, lies here, having had one husband for thirty-eight years.
EDCS-67200405.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Zosime, a widow and servant of Christ, who was married for thirty-eight years.

EDCS-67200406.translation: To Romanā, most orderly, fond of husband and children, honored, who lived well for twenty years, the earth now covers her here.
EDCS-67200406.summary: This is a funerary epigram for Romanā, who lived twenty years and was known for her domestic virtues.

EDCS-67200407.translation: Patrōeinos, faithful servant of Christ, lies here, aged twenty-five years.
EDCS-67200407.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Patrōeinos, a servant of Christ who died at the age of twenty-five.

EDCS-67200408.translation: To you alone, Lord and Savior of bodies and souls, may you yourself receive into the bosom of Abraham the soul of Hilarion, who sojourned in this world for three years, and keep him, O philanthropic one, for into your hands he commended his spirit.
EDCS-67200408.summary: This is a funerary prayer for Hilarion, a child who died at the age of three, entrusted to God's care.

EDCS-67200410.translation: The passing of Gerasimos, hieromonk of Paga.
EDCS-67200410.summary: This inscription records the death of Gerasimos, a hieromonk.

EDCS-67200411.translation: In the year 100, during the priesthood of Maximō, Khyuakhi 16, Lyōn, son of Androkleēs, aged 21 years.
EDCS-67200411.summary: This is a funerary inscription, dated by the year 100 of an unspecified era and mentioning the priest Maximō, for Lyōn who died at 21.

EDCS-67200413.translation: Lucius Furius Secundus, soldier of the first Italian legion, aged sixty years.
EDCS-67200413.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Lucius Furius Secundus, a soldier of the Legio I Italica, who died at the age of sixty.

EDCS-67200630.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Nerva Traianus Augustus, son of the deified Nerva, grandson of the deified Traianus, Traianus Hadrianus Augustus, chief priest, holder of tribunician power for the 26th time, father of the fatherland, the city of Eleutherna.
EDCS-67200630.summary: This is a dedicatory inscription from the city of Eleutherna honoring Emperor Hadrian during his 26th year of tribunician power.

EDCS-67200631.translation: Euphratas the most holy bishop builds this divine precinct, a stately house, raising it to Michael the Archangel, whose intercessions may you grant us grace.
EDCS-67200631.summary: This inscription records the construction of a church dedicated to Michael the Archangel by Bishop Euphratas.

EDCS-67200633.translation: To Caesar the god, son of the god, Augustus.
EDCS-67200633.summary: This is a dedicatory inscription to the deified Emperor Caesar Augustus.

EDCS-67200805.translation: Lianōs, son of Dionysios, having served as agoranomos, to Hermes, the bringer of abundant fruit.
EDCS-67200805.summary: This is a dedication to Hermes, the god of commerce and abundance, by Lianōs, son of Dionysios, who served as agoranomos (market supervisor).

EDCS-67200806.translation: To the Most High God, a vow. Tiberius Claudius Cataplus.
EDCS-67200806.summary: This is a votive inscription to a deity, likely by Tiberius Claudius Cataplus.

EDCS-67200807.translation: In the time of Betranius, our most holy and blessed Archbishop, the work of the mosaic was renewed.
EDCS-67200807.summary: This inscription records the renovation of a mosaic, dedicated during the tenure of Archbishop Betranius.

EDCS-67500029.translation: Emperor Caesar Vespasian Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 3rd time, imperator for the 8th time, father of the fatherland, consul for the 3rd time, designated consul for the 4th time, through his legate Quintus Paconius Agrippinus, restored the gardens for the Roman people.
EDCS-67500029.summary: This inscription records Emperor Vespasian, through his legate, restoring gardens, likely during his reign (c. 69-79 AD).

EDCS-77400161.translation: Gnaeus Cornelius Scipio, son of Gnaeus, the Spaniard, benefactor of the city.
EDCS-77400161.summary: This is a dedicatory inscription honoring Gnaeus Cornelius Scipio, a Spaniard and benefactor of the city.

EDCS-77400189.translation: To the Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Nerva, Nerva Trajan Augustus, Germanicus, Dacicus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 16th time, consul for the 6th time, father of the fatherland, the city of Lytton, to the founder of the inhabited world, through the protokosmos Tiberius Claudius Boinobius.
EDCS-77400189.summary: This inscription is a dedication by the city of Lytton to Emperor Trajan, praising him as the 'founder of the inhabited world,' likely dating to Trajan's reign (98-117 AD).

EDCS-77400215.translation: To Imperator Caesar Galerius Valerius Maximianus, the most distinguished and courageous Caesar, lord of himself and of the inhabited world, Marcus Aurelius Byzes, the most distinguished governor of Crete.
EDCS-77400215.summary: This inscription is a dedication by Marcus Aurelius Byzes, governor of Crete, to Caesar Galerius Valerius Maximianus, likely during the Tetrarchy (c. 293-305 AD).

EDCS-77500072.translation: To Oecumenius Dositheus Asclepiodotus, the most illustrious consular, the council of Oluntium, his benefactor, for Nike of Rome, and for his homeland, Ursus dedicated to him a temple of Eudikia and of the ruling family.
EDCS-77500072.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a temple by Ursus to Oecumenius Dositheus Asclepiodotus, a distinguished consular, and to Nike of Rome and his homeland, by the council of Oluntium.

EDCS-78000038.translation: ... for condemnation if anyone ever attempts to take this grain or its value for any other need, but not for the sustenance of the city itself. But the records of transactions contain this in detail, and how much Serapammon received in gold and how much in grain, so that the endowment of the metropolis might always increase through timely purchase and just resale. He dedicated this column to the sacred and divine images of the common lords and rulers, the Augusti Flavius Gratianus, Flavius Valentinianus, and Flavius Theodosius, an immortal memory of their reign, and through this means, wishing to preserve the nation of the Cretans, the records concerning this matter were drawn up before the 18th day before the Kalends of July, during the consulship of Syagrius and Eucherius.
EDCS-78000038.summary: This inscription, dating to the consulship of Syagrius and Eucherius (381 AD), concerns the management of grain funds for the city of Crete, with a dedication to the Augusti Gratian, Valentinian, and Theodosius.

EDCS-78000039.translation: To the invincible victors and eternal rulers of the inhabited world, the pious Augusti Gratianus, Valentinianus, and Theodosius, before the entrance of the new praetorium, which is being constructed from the foundations, Oecumenius Dositheus Asclepiodotus, the most illustrious consular of the province of Crete, established it, dedicating himself to their piety.
EDCS-78000039.summary: This inscription records Oecumenius Dositheus Asclepiodotus, the consular governor of Crete, dedicating a new praetorium to the Augusti Gratian, Valentinian, and Theodosius, likely in the late 4th century AD.

EDCS-78300475.translation: To King Ptolemy Philopator and Queen Arsinoë, the water supply and the Nymphaeum. Lucius Gaius, Roman, commander of the garrison.
EDCS-78300475.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a water supply and a Nymphaeum to King Ptolemy Philopator and Queen Arsinoë, by Lucius Gaius, a Roman garrison commander.

EDCS-78300601.translation: To Gnaeus Cornelius Lentulus Marcellinus, son of Publius, legate, proconsul, the patron and saviour, the Cyreneans.
EDCS-78300601.summary: An honorary inscription dedicated by the people of Cyrene to Cornelius Lentulus Marcellinus, a Roman official, likely in the 1st century BCE or 1st century CE.

EDCS-78300606.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrianus, son of the deified Trajan Parthicus, grandson of the deified Nerva, Augustus, Imperator for the second time, chief priest, holder of tribunician power for the 22nd time, consul for the third time, father of the fatherland, saviour and founder, and to the Emperor Titus Aelius Caesar Antoninus, son of Hadrianus Augustus; the city of Cyrene, adorned by him and his statues.
EDCS-78300606.summary: An inscription honoring Emperors Hadrian and Antoninus Pius, set up by the city of Cyrene, likely in the mid-2nd century CE.

EDCS-78500075.summary: A fragmentary inscription mentioning Claudius Acilius Cleobulus and funds, its purpose and date are unclear.

EDCS-78500076.translation: Anicius Paulinus, most distinguished, former proconsul and former prefect of the imperial city Rome; by decree of the common assembly of the whole province, Eumenius Dositheus Asclepiodotus, most distinguished, ex-consul of the province of Crete, I have set up.
EDCS-78500076.summary: An inscription by Eumenius Dositheus Asclepiodotus, an ex-consul of Crete, honoring Anicius Paulinus, a former high-ranking Roman official, likely from the 3rd century CE.

EDCS-78500078.translation: Probianus, most distinguished and most magnificent, former prefect of the imperial city Rome; by decree of the common assembly of the province of Crete, Eumenius Dositheus Asclepiodotus, most distinguished, ex-consul, I have set up.
EDCS-78500078.summary: An inscription by Eumenius Dositheus Asclepiodotus, an ex-consul of Crete, honoring Probianus, a former Roman prefect, likely from the 3rd century CE.

EDCS-78500079.translation: To Eumenius Dositheus Asclepiodotus, most distinguished ex-consul, for his virtue and justice, Aemilius Quintilius Pyrrhus, the sophist, and Ulpius Fursidius Panhellenius, the leading citizen, by decree of the common assembly of the province of Crete, near the Dike, have set up.
EDCS-78500079.summary: An inscription honoring Eumenius Dositheus Asclepiodotus, an ex-consul of Crete, for his virtue and justice, erected by a sophist and a leading citizen, likely in the 3rd century CE.

EDCS-78500080.translation: By divine decree and by decree of the province of Crete, Petronius Probus, most distinguished and most excellent, former consul, prefect of the praetorium, the benefactor and saviour of the nation; Flavius Fursidius Aristides, most distinguished ex-consul of the province of Crete, has set up.
EDCS-78500080.summary: An inscription honoring Petronius Probus, a former praetorian prefect, by Flavius Fursidius Aristides, an ex-consul of Crete, likely in the 3rd century CE.

EDCS-78700095.translation: Numisius Marcellianus, proconsul, has dedicated to Artemis from the offerings placed for her, through the priest Tiberius Claudius Battus, with the assistance of the weights: 17 litrae, 8 unciae for Battus, son of Lucius Antonius Secundus, neocoros of the priestesses of Artemis, as follows: Claudia Arata, 3 unciae; Munatia Arata, 5 unciae, 8 obols; Olympias Carneades, 5 unciae; Antonia Procla, 6 unciae; Ancharena Timō, 8 unciae; Claudia Faustina, 6 unciae; Telesō daughter of Hegesius, 6 unciae; Ancharena Calibōla, 5 unciae; Claudia Procla, 3 unciae; Megō daughter of Euphranor, 5 unciae; Claudia Faunilla, 6 unciae; and 10 unciae, 4 litrae for meat; Olympias daughter of Theophilus, 3 unciae; Anaxō daughter of Euphranor, 3 unciae; Bedía Mareina, 3 unciae; Flavia Cydimachē, 4 unciae; Julia Cleomēda, 10 unciae; Megō daughter of Euboulos, 4 unciae; Flavia Neicippa, 3 unciae; Julia Flaccilla, 1 litra; Flavia Justinilla, 10 unciae; Sestia Faustilla, 5 unciae; Velidria, 10 unciae; Battus for the city, 6 unciae; sacrificial offering, a deer and a cauldron, 1 litra.
EDCS-78700095.summary: An extensive list of offerings and their weights dedicated to Artemis by the proconsul Numisius Marcellianus, likely in the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-78700097.summary: A very brief inscription identifying Vestalis as a proconsul, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-78800226.translation: Under Kosmos, with Lasthēnes son of Sōsamenos from Peisō.
EDCS-78800226.summary: A fragmentary inscription mentioning a Kosmos and Lasthēnes, possibly related to a list of officials or a dedication in a Greek context.

EDCS-82300011.translation: Emperor Caesar Augustus, high priest, holding tribunician power for the seventeenth time, emperor for the fourteenth time, states: Since I find that all Romans in the province of Cyrene possess property valued at fifteen hundred denarii or more, among whom are the judges, and that among these there are certain conspiracies, the ambassadors from the cities of the province complained about the oppressive measures affecting the Greeks in the death sentences of those same people, who accused and testified against each other; and I myself have realized that some are unjustly burdened in this way and have been brought to the ultimate punishment, until the Senate deliberates on this matter or I myself find a better solution, it seems to me that those who will govern the provinces of Crete and Cyrene will do well and appropriately by setting forth in the province of Cyrene the same number of Greek judges as Romans, from those with the highest property assessments, with no one younger than twenty-five years old, neither Roman nor Greek, nor possessing less property and wealth, if there is a sufficiency of such men, valued at seven thousand five hundred denarii, or if the number of those liable cannot be filled in this way, they shall appoint judges possessing half of this assessment or less. Let them appoint judges in the death trials of the Greeks. If a Greek, when being tried, on the day before the accuser begins to speak, is given the power to choose whether he wants all judges to be Roman or half Greek, he shall choose half Greek. Then, when the spheres are raised and their names are inscribed, from one urn the names of the Romans, and from the other the names of the Greeks, let them draw lots until twenty-five of each group are selected. The prosecutor may then select one from each group, and the defendant three from all of them, by which he will not choose all Romans or all Greeks. Then let all the others be dismissed for voting and cast their votes: Romans separately into one box, and Greeks separately into another. Then, after the votes from each side have been counted separately, the general shall openly announce whatever the majority of all has decided. And since the relatives of those who have perished do not often leave the unjust deaths unavenged, and it is likely that there will not be a lack of Greek accusers for those liable, who will properly and appropriately seek justice for the deceased relatives or citizens, it seems to me that those who will govern Crete and Cyrene will do well if, in the province of Cyrene, for the murder of a Greek man or woman, they do not admit a Roman accuser of a Greek, unless someone honored by Roman citizenship is prosecuting for the death of one of his relatives or fellow citizens. Emperor Caesar Augustus, high priest, holding tribunician power for the seventeenth time, states: Envy and slander do not owe anything to Publius Sextius Sceva, because he stated that he knew and wished to report what pertained to my salvation and the public affairs, and that these men, Aulus Stlaccius Maximus, son of Lucius Stlaccius, and Lucius Stlaccius Macedo, son of Lucius, and Publius Lacuntanius, freedman of Publius Philero, had been sent to me in chains from the province of Cyrene; for Sestius took care of this fittingly and diligently. Furthermore, since they know nothing of what pertains to me and public affairs, and they stated that they had been deceived in the province and had lied, they made it clear to me. Having been released from custody, I am letting them go. But I forbid Aulus Stlaccius Maximus, whom the ambassadors of Cyrene accuse of having removed statues from public places, including the one to which the city inscribed my name, to leave without my order until I rule on this matter. Emperor Caesar Augustus, high priest, holding tribunician power for the seventeenth time, states: I order that those citizens from the province of Cyrene who have been honored shall serve in rotation in the body of Greeks, with no less obligation than others, except for those to whom exemption from service has been granted by law or a decree of the Senate, or by the judgment of my father, or by my own decree, along with their citizenship, and that these themselves, to whom the exemption has been granted, shall be exempt from these affairs. It is my pleasure that they pay for acquired properties. Emperor Caesar Augustus, high priest, holding tribunician power for the seventeenth time, states: As for any disputes that arise between Greeks in the province of Cyrene, after the subordinate cases have been heard, the one who holds the province shall decide and rule on them himself, or provide a council of judges. For all other matters, it is pleasing that Greek judges be provided, unless someone demanding or being held accountable wishes to have Roman judges. And for those for whom Greek judges will be provided under this my decree, it is not pleasing that a judge be provided from that city from which the accuser or the one being held accountable originates, nor from the city of the one demanding or being held accountable. Emperor Caesar Augustus, chief priest, holding tribunician power for the 19th time, states: The decree of the Senate, ratified in the consulship of Gaius Calvisius and Lucius Passienus, with my presence and co-signing, concerning the safety of the allies of the Roman people, so that it may be known to all how much we care, I have decided to send to the provinces and subject it to my edict, from which it will be clear to all inhabitants of the provinces how much care I and the Senate take that none of those subject to us suffer or be extorted anything unjustly. Decree of the Senate, concerning matters about which Gaius Calvisius Sabinus and Lucius Passienus Rufus, consuls, made reports, which our leader Emperor Caesar Augustus wished to be brought before the council through us, from the council by lot, concerning the safety of the allies of the Roman people. It was pleasing to the Senate that our ancestors legislated judgments for the recovery of money, so that the allies might more easily pursue and recover money for themselves for wrongs they have suffered, and be able to regain what has been taken away. Since this type of trial is sometimes very burdensome and unpleasant for those for whom the law was written, and for the provincials who are far away, and it is burdensome to summon poor witnesses and those who are weak due to illness or old age, it is pleasing to the Senate that if any of the allies, after this decree of the Senate has been made, wish to claim money, whether public or private, without the need to hold the recipient accountable for the principal sum, and appear before one of the magistrates who has the authority to convene the Senate, that magistrate shall bring them before the Senate as soon as possible, and provide an advocate to speak on their behalf in the Senate, whom they shall request. However, one to whom the law provides an exemption from this service shall not be compelled to be an advocate. Those against whom accusations are brought in the Senate shall, so that a magistrate who grants them access to the Senate may decide on the same day, with the Senate present, be chosen by lot from all ex-consuls who are in Rome itself or within twenty miles of the city, four; similarly, from all ex-praetors in Rome itself or within twenty miles of the city, three; similarly, from all other senators or those who have the right to express their opinion in the Senate, two. No one shall be chosen who is seventy years of age or older, or who is holding office or authority, or is a supervisor of a court, or a curator of the grain supply, or who is prevented by illness from performing this service, having sworn before the Senate and given three men of the Senate as sureties for this, or who is related by kinship or closeness of ties to the person being prosecuted, so as to testify in a public court according to the Julian law regarding trials, not be compelled unwillingly; or the one being prosecuted swears in the Senate that he considers him an enemy, but no more than three shall swear. Those who are chosen in this manner, nine in number, the magistrate who makes the selection shall ensure that within two days, those who are pursuing the claims and from whom they are pursuing them, are selected in turn until five remain. If any of these judges dies before the case is decided, or if some other reason prevents him from judging, and his excuse is approved, then five men from the Senate shall swear, and then the magistrate, in the presence of the judges and those pursuing the claims, and the person from whom the claims are being pursued, shall draw by lot from among those men who are of the same rank and have held the same offices as the one who is replaced, to fill the position, on the condition that he will not draw a man whom it is not permitted to draw according to this decree of the Senate against the one being prosecuted. And the elected judges shall hear and decide only on those matters for which someone is held accountable for misappropriating public or private funds, and they shall order them to return the amount of money that the accountable persons prove they have misappropriated, either privately or publicly, within thirty days of the judges' decision. Those who are necessary to decide on these matters and express their opinion shall be excused from all public service until they have decided and expressed their opinion, except for sacred and public services. It is pleasing to the Senate that the magistrate who makes the selection of the judges, or if he is unable, the consuls, shall preside over the proceedings of this trial and grant authority to witnesses in Italy to announce, with the provision that the one pursuing a private claim may announce no more than five, and the one pursuing a public claim no more than ten. It is similarly pleasing to the Senate that the judges who are chosen by this decree shall openly declare their opinions, and what the majority decides shall be allowed.
EDCS-82300011.summary: This is a lengthy imperial rescript from Emperor Augustus detailing procedures for judicial processes in the provinces of Crete and Cyrene, particularly concerning the rights of Greeks and Romans in legal disputes and death penalty cases, and also includes a senatorial decree regarding the recovery of funds owed to allies of Rome.

EDCS-82300032.translation: Petronius Probus, the most distinguished, formerly consul and formerly praetorian prefect, has erected this by decree of the most distinguished council of Gortyn. Oecumenius Dositheus Asclepiodotus, the most distinguished, formerly consular, erected it.
EDCS-82300032.summary: This inscription records the erection of a monument to Petronius Probus, a distinguished former consul and praetorian prefect, by a decree of the council of Gortyn, honoring him for his distinguished status.

EDCS-82300033.translation: To the incorruptible Justice, the incorruptible prefect, the divine Aristides erected this statue, persuaded by the vote of the chosen men and by the decree of the council, giving a few things in return for many good deeds.
EDCS-82300033.summary: This inscription records the erection of a statue to Justice by Aristides, an incorruptible prefect, at the behest of the council and chosen men, honoring Justice for its role in rewarding good deeds.

EDCS-82300034.translation: Flavius Hypatius, the most distinguished, formerly consul and formerly prefect of the praetorium, has erected this by decree of the common council of the entire province. Oecumenius Dositheus Asclepiodotus, the most distinguished, formerly consular of the province of Crete, erected it.
EDCS-82300034.summary: This inscription records the erection of a monument to Flavius Hypatius, a distinguished former consul and praetorian prefect, by the common council of the province of Crete, with Oecumenius Dositheus Asclepiodotus, a former consular, as the erector.

EDCS-82300035.translation: Of all the western land, the mighty ruler Marcellinus, treasurer of the governors, a glorious scion of Hellas, who by his just governance and aid made cities lighter and more bearable. For this reason, the council and Pyrrhus erected him as a witness to Justice and laws at the entrance. In charge.
EDCS-82300035.summary: This is a dedicatory inscription honoring Marcellinus, described as a ruler, treasurer, and scion of Hellas, for his just governance that lightened the burden of cities, erected by the council and Pyrrhus as a testament to justice and laws.
